steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes a threshold: if a pollinator's maximum positive charge is at least as high as foraging honeybees', it can facilitate electrostatic pollen transfer. The blank must be completed with a conclusion that follows from matching the honeybee charge against other pollinators shown in the graph.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'houseflies should demonstrate the opposite tendency.': "Opposite tendency" means houseflies should fail to facilitate transfer, but the graph shows houseflies have a higher maximum charge than honeybees — so they should also be capable, not incapable. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Anna’s hummingbirds should also demonstrate this capacity.': The graph shows Anna's hummingbirds have a maximum positive charge equal to or greater than foraging honeybees. Since the honeybee level is sufficient for electrostatic transfer, hummingbirds at that level should also demonstrate the same capacity. ✓
  • Choice C — 'the minimum positive charge necessary for a pollinator to induce electrostatic attraction must be greater than 100 pC.': The text says the honeybee charge is "sufficiently high" — it does not establish a precise numerical minimum threshold, and the graph does not support this specific claim. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Anna’s hummingbirds and houseflies, having greater maximum charges than foraging honeybees have, should not demonstrate this capacity.': This reverses the logic entirely. Having a greater charge than the sufficient threshold means they should demonstrate the capacity, not fail to. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
